author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
The Accusations in the Seventh Item, Which Pelagius Confessed.
title: The Accusations in the Seventh Item, Which Pelagius Confessed.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Let us now see what were the two points out of all that were alleged which Pelagius was unwilling to anathematize, and admitted to be his own opinions, but to remove their offensive aspect explained in what sense he held them.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: “That a man,” says he, “is able to be without sin has been asserted already.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Asserted no doubt, and we remember the assertion quite well; but still it was mitigated, and approved by the judges, in that God’s grace was added, concerning which nothing was said in the original draft of his doctrine.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Touching the second, however, of these points, we ought to pay careful attention to what he said in answer to the charge against him.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: “Concerning the fact, indeed,” says he, “that before the Lord’s coming there were persons without sin, we now again assert that previous to Christ’s advent some men lived holy and righteous lives, according to the teaching of the sacred Scriptures.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: He did not dare to say: “We now again assert that previous to Christ’s advent there were persons without sin,” although this had been laid to his charge after the very words of Cœlestius.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: For he perceived how dangerous such a statement was, and into what trouble it would bring him.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: So he reduced the sentence to these harmless dimensions: “We again assert that before the coming of Christ there were persons who led holy and righteous lives.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Of course there were: who would deny it?

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: But to say this is a very different thing from saying that they lived “without sin.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: Because, indeed, those ancient worthies lived holy and righteous lives, they could for that very reason better confess: “If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: In the present day, also, many men live holy and righteous lives; but yet it is no untruth they utter when in their prayer they say: “Forgive us our debts, even as we forgive our debtors.”

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: This avowal was accordingly acceptable to the judges, in the sense in which Pelagius solemnly declared his belief; but certainly not in the sense which Cœlestius, according to the original charge against him, was said to hold.

verses.book: A Work on the Proceedings of Pelagius.
verses.text: We must now treat in detail of the topics which still remain, to the best of our ability.
